effect_description: Dramatically reduces sebum production (up to 90% by sebocyte apoptosis), normalizes keratinization, reduces C. acnes colonization, and has anti-inflammatory effects. Single course often curative (65-85% long-term remission).
Effective for severe hormonal acne unresponsive to other treatments. Permanently shrinks sebaceous glands regardless of hormonal driver.
Expected side effect. >80% develop dry skin due to dramatic sebum reduction. Managed with emollient-rich moisturizers.
20-30% develop photosensitivity. Mandatory broad-spectrum SPF 50+ and sun avoidance. Physical sunscreens preferred.
Category X - absolute contraindication. Causes severe birth defects (craniofacial, cardiac, CNS). iPLEDGE mandatory in US.
Normalize keratinocyte proliferation and differentiation, prevent comedone formation, stimulate comedone extrusion. First-line for comedonal and mild inflammatory acne.
Stimulate collagen synthesis (types I and III) via TGF-β, increase angiogenesis, disperse melanin, and inhibit MMPs. Gold standard for photoaging.
Accelerates cell turnover and melanin dispersion. Tretinoin particularly effective for PIH in darker skin types when combined with sunscreen.
Increases epidermal turnover and disperses melanosomes. Triple combination (tretinoin + hydroquinone + corticosteroid) is gold standard for melasma.
Tretinoin Category C (avoid if possible); Tazarotene Category X (absolute contraindication); Adapalene Category C (newer data suggests minimal systemic absorption).
FDA-approved for acne: norgestimate, drospirenone, norethindrone combinations. Reduce ovarian androgen production and increase SHBG. Effective for cyclical jawline acne.
citation: medication-skin-interactions.md: Section 5; clinical-guidelines.md: Acne hormonal therapy

effect_description: Increase SHBG → reduce free testosterone → decrease androgenic effects on hair follicles. Drospirenone-containing pills particularly effective.
Estrogen stimulates melanogenesis. Can trigger or worsen melasma, especially in sun-exposed individuals with genetic predisposition.
Anti-androgen that blocks androgen receptors and inhibits 5α-reductase. 50-200 mg/day effective for adult female acne, especially jawline/chin distribution.
citation: medication-skin-interactions.md: Section 6; clinical-guidelines.md: Acne hormonal therapy

effect_description: Blocks androgen receptors in hair follicles. Reduces terminal hair growth in androgen-sensitive areas.
Anti-androgen effects may slow progression of female pattern hair loss. Often combined with minoxidil.
Absolutely contraindicated - anti-androgenic, can feminize male fetus. Strict contraception required.
Potent anti-inflammatory. Reduces IL-4, IL-13, TNF-α, and other Th2 cytokines. Rapidly controls flares. First-line for acute AD.
Reduces inflammation and keratinocyte hyperproliferation. Rapid onset. Combined with vitamin D analogs for maintenance.
Low-potency corticosteroids reduce inflammation in seborrheic dermatitis. Often combined with antifungals.
Topical corticosteroids on face cause steroid rosacea, perioral dermatitis, and skin atrophy. NEVER use high-potency steroids on face.
Universal with prolonged use. Downregulates glucocorticoid receptors, reduces collagen synthesis, causes striae and easy bruising.
Systemic and topical steroids can cause steroid acne (monomorphic papules/pustules). Also causes perioral dermatitis.
Topical: Generally safe with appropriate use (prefer low potency). Systemic: Category C/D - possible cleft lip/palate risk (weak evidence), third trimester adrenal suppression risk.
Reduces CoQ10 synthesis (20-40% reduction) → mitochondrial dysfunction in skin cells → impaired repair, barrier disruption, dry skin (5-15% incidence).
1-5% develop photosensitivity, possibly related to porphyrin metabolism disruption. SPF 30+ recommended.
Category X. Cholesterol essential for fetal development. Discontinue immediately if pregnancy occurs.
Most common side effect (5-20% SSRIs, higher with SNRIs). Altered hypothalamic thermoregulation and serotonin receptor activation on eccrine glands.
2-8% develop photosensitivity. Mechanism unclear but serotonin receptors on melanocytes may be involved. SPF 50+ recommended.
Anticholinergic effects of some agents reduce sweating and can cause dry skin (2-5%).
Can exacerbate psoriasis, especially COX-2 selective agents. Arachidonic acid shunting to lipoxygenase pathway increases leukotrienes.
Prostaglandin depletion impairs granulation tissue formation, angiogenesis, and epithelialization. Clinically significant in surgical patients.
1-5% photosensitivity, especially piroxicam and ketoprofen. SPF 50+ recommended.
Third trimester: Category D (premature ductus arteriosus closure, oligohydramnios). First/second trimester: avoid if possible.
>80% develop anagen effluvium. Taxanes, anthracyclines, and alkylating agents most commonly cause hair loss. Usually reversible 3-6 months post-treatment.
EGFR inhibitors cause severe xerosis (30-50%). Chemotherapy impairs keratinocyte proliferation and barrier repair.
Virtually all chemotherapy agents are Category D/X. Fetal death, malformations, growth restriction. Mandatory contraception during and after treatment.
Improves insulin sensitivity → reduces IGF-1/mTORC1 signaling → decreases sebum production. Particularly effective in PCOS-related acne.
Improves insulin sensitivity → reduces ovarian androgen production → decreases hirsutism in PCOS.
Anti-inflammatory and antibacterial effects. Reduce C. acnes and inhibit MMPs. Maximum 3-4 months to limit antibiotic exposure.
Doxycycline 40mg modified release (anti-inflammatory dose) effective for papulopustular rosacea. Reduces inflammation without full antibacterial effect.
Especially doxycycline. Photosensitivity reactions common. Mandatory SPF 50+ and sun avoidance.
Category D. Contraindicated - affects fetal teeth and bone development. Also contraindicated in children <8 years.
High-dose biotin (>5-10mg/day) can cause acneiform eruptions and worsen existing acne. Mechanism unclear.
Benzoyl peroxide degrades tretinoin (oxidizes it). Do not apply simultaneously. OK with adapalene (stable). If using together: BPO AM, retinoid PM.
Additive toxicity risk - hypervitaminosis A. Can cause intracranial hypertension, liver toxicity, bone changes. Avoid all vitamin A supplements during isotretinoin therapy.
Combined use increases risk of pseudotumor cerebri (benign intracranial hypertension) - headache, visual changes, papilledema. Emergency.
Hyperkalemia risk. Both affect potassium balance. Monitor potassium if combined; often avoid co-prescription.
Serotonin syndrome risk - diaphoresis, flushing, hyperthermia, tremor, agitation. Medical emergency. Includes MAOIs, tramadol, triptans, St. John's Wort.
Both affect skin barrier and wound healing. Using together on same area increases irritation and atrophy risk. Space applications or rotate.
Increase bleeding risk and impair wound healing. Discontinue 7-10 days before invasive procedures (coordinate with prescriber).
Statins deplete CoQ10 (20-40% reduction). CoQ10 supplementation (100-200mg/day) recommended to counter mitochondrial dysfunction and myalgia. May benefit skin.
Live vaccines contraindicated due to impaired immune response and risk of disseminated infection. Includes MMR, varicella, yellow fever, intranasal flu.
Increased skin cancer risk with combined immunosuppression and UV exposure. Monitor closely; consider alternatives.
